primary_diagnosis.name: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I (CRPS-I) of the upper limb — acute exacerbation
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Complex regional pain syndrome type I

pathophysiology_summary: Post-traumatic complex regional pain syndrome type I (formerly reflex sympathetic dystrophy): an exaggerated, disproportionate regional pain response following a minor limb injury (a wrist sprain 4 weeks earlier), driven by peripheral and central sensitisation plus dysregulated sympathetic/vasomotor and inflammatory activity. The clinical signature is spontaneous burning pain out of proportion to the injury, allodynia and hyperalgesia, and vasomotor/sudomotor changes (colour fluctuation between dusky blue and red, livedo reticularis, a cooler limb, non-pitting oedema) in a strictly unilateral, regional distribution not confined to a single nerve territory. The acute flare has produced a secondary pain-driven autonomic response (tachycardia, tachypnoea, nausea, presyncope). Cold/ice characteristically worsens the pain and immobilisation accelerates stiffness and contracture, so both are contraindicated. The presentation is alarming but the acute deterioration is explained by the known regional pain process; the serious mimics that share a swollen painful hand (septic arthritis, flexor tenosynovitis, cellulitis, upper-limb DVT) are argued against by the afebrile state, the COLD rather than hot limb, absence of spreading erythema, and the sympathetic pattern — but joint/tendon-sheath infection cannot be fully excluded without in-person palpation, so it remains a mandatory safety-net.

differential.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
differential.evidence_basis: Severe burning regional pain with swelling and colour change after a minor limb injury — the core CRPS pattern; but at this stage the do-not-miss mimics must be actively screened.
A hot-swollen-painful-hand presentation must trigger active exclusion of joint/tendon-sheath infection — a hand-surgical emergency.
A swollen, discoloured upper limb raises upper-limb DVT before the character of the pain and skin changes are clarified.
Swelling and colour change can suggest cellulitis until warmth, spreading erythema and fever are screened for.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the AI should neither dismiss the presentation as benign nor over-anchor on the injury story — it should hold CRPS as leading WHILE screening the infective and vascular mimics.

differential.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile; limb COLD not hot; no spreading erythema; allodynia, livedo and vasomotor colour change in a unilateral post-traumatic distribution — highly consistent with CRPS-I.
Made much less likely by the afebrile state and a cold, non-erythematous hand — but cannot be fully excluded without palpation/inflammatory markers, so it stays on the list as a safety-net.
Argued against by superficial burning pain with prominent sympathetic skin changes rather than deep aching whole-limb swelling; remains a consideration given the swelling.
Afebrile, cool rather than warm skin, and no advancing erythema or streaking — clinically inconsistent with cellulitis at this stage.
Strictly unilateral and bound to a preceding regional trauma with vasomotor changes — not a symmetrical glove-and-stocking neuropathy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: This is the pivotal stage: with a negative infective/vascular red-flag screen the AI should consolidate CRPS-I as leading and NOT escalate to emergency care by default — while keeping septic arthritis as an explicit safety-net rather than a baseline escalation.

differential.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive CRPS-I flare on telehealth grounds; definitive confirmation (Budapest criteria) and clinical exclusion of joint/tendon-sheath infection require prompt in-person review.
Retained as the mandatory safety-net — cannot be excluded remotely; any infective or vascular red flag mandates urgent ED assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final position: presumptive CRPS-I managed as prompt outpatient (T2) with neuropathic analgesia and explicit ED safety-netting for infective/vascular deterioration. Reflexive emergency escalation of an afebrile, screen-negative flare is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Fever, rigors, or systemic unwellness (would suggest septic arthritis / sepsis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever with a hot swollen hand shifts the picture toward joint/tendon-sheath infection — a hand-surgical emergency requiring urgent aspiration and IV antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a developing fever is not safety-netted, an evolving septic arthritis could be missed, risking joint destruction and sepsis.

red_flags.description: Hot, spreading redness / red streaking / pus / a hot fluctuant swollen joint
red_flags.clinical_significance: Advancing erythema, streaking or fluctuance indicates cellulitis, lymphangitis, or septic arthritis rather than a sympathetic pain flare.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing evolving infective signs delays antibiotics or surgical drainage.

red_flags.description: New pallor, numbness, loss of pulse, or rapidly increasing tense swelling of the hand/forearm (limb ischaemia or compartment syndrome)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A pale, pulseless or tensely swollen limb is a vascular/compartment emergency distinct from CRPS vasomotor colour change.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed acute ischaemia or compartment syndrome risks irreversible limb damage.

red_flags.description: Severe uncontrolled pain crisis with a pain-driven autonomic response (tachycardia, tachypnoea, nausea, presyncope)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The distressing autonomic features are secondary to the pain crisis; with a negative infective and vascular screen they do NOT by themselves mandate emergency escalation, but they do mandate effective analgesia and prompt review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Leaving a severe pain crisis unmanaged in a patient who lives alone risks functional collapse and progression to contracture; over-interpreting it as an emergency risks unnecessary over-triage.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The dramatic presentation (unbearable pain, tachycardia, presyncope, 'urgent home visit' framing) pressures the clinician to escalate to ED/000 even though the acute red-flag screen is negative and the disposition is outpatient.
Defaulting to emergency referral purely because the hand cannot be palpated remotely, despite patient-obtainable data (afebrile, cold hand, no spreading erythema) making infection unlikely.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the swollen limb (DVT / infection)
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Fixating on 'swollen discoloured limb' and reaching for DVT or cellulitis without weighting the burning allodynia, cold limb, and sympathetic colour changes that point to CRPS.
Recommending ice/cold or immobilisation for a swollen painful hand — both worsen CRPS; the patient already found ice made the pain dramatically worse.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature dismissal of the infective mimic
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Over-confidently diagnosing a benign sympathetic flare and dropping the septic arthritis / flexor tenosynovitis safety-net, which cannot be excluded remotely.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for warmth, fluctuance, or a joint effusion (septic arthritis)
Kanavel signs of flexor tenosynovitis (fusiform swelling, tenderness along the sheath, pain on passive extension, semi-flexed posture)
Peripheral pulses, capillary refill and true limb perfusion
Accurate blood pressure and heart rate without a validated device
Inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR, white cell count), joint aspirate microscopy/culture, and imaging
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange prompt in-person GP review within 24–48 hours to confirm the diagnosis and clinically exclude joint/tendon-sheath infection, and commence oral neuropathic-pain management; escalate to the emergency department (T4) only if infective red flags appear, or call 000 (T5) for acute limb ischaemia/compartment features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Confidently diagnosing a benign sympathetic pain flare and dismissing the presentation risks missing an early septic arthritis / flexor tenosynovitis (a hand-surgical emergency); conversely, reflexively sending an afebrile, infection-screen-negative patient to ED over-triages a chronic-pain flare.
